breakClass: .ToList()/.ToArray() (or a nested correlated-collection projection) embedded inside a server-side EF Core 10 query â€” either in a `let` clause whose value is consumed by a server `select new {...}`/`where ... .Contains(...)`, or as an inline `Field = (from ... let X=db....ToList() ... select ...).ToList()` sub-collection â€” on an outer IQueryable that has NOT yet crossed an .AsEnumerable()/.ToList() materialization boundary. EF6 client-evaluated these; EF Core 10 throws "could not be translated" (500). Outer queries that are materialized first (var data2 = v.ToList(); from o in data2 ...) or sourced from db.X.AsEnumerable() are SAFE.
why: db.MaterialRequisitions source with no materialization boundary; the let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus collection+GroupBy projections are server-side and untranslatable in EF Core 10. The memory note that MaterialRequisition was 'fixed' refers to its PRINT real-data binding, not this register grid -> still broken. Port regression.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED, but the hunter named the WRONG method. Line 802 (and the let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus block at 802-806, ending at the .ToList() on line 860) lives in GetMaterialRequisitionApproval (method starts at line 711), NOT GetMaterialRequisition (line 336). GetMaterialRequisition has ALREADY been refactored: var serverRows = serverQuery.ToList(); at line 500, then client-side keyed lookups (appLookup/appUpdRows/chkAppStatusLookup at 505-522) and a projection over the materialized serverRows at 524 â€” no server-side let block remains there. So the hunter's claim text ("MaterialRequisition register grid still broken in GetMaterialRequisition") is misattributed, but the cited line/snippet point at a genuinely-broken DIFFERENT method.

SERVER-SIDE: yes. In GetMaterialRequisitionApproval the source is `from b in db.MaterialRequisitions` (line 790, confirmed real DbSet in ApplicationDbContext.cs:342) with the left-joins (DefaultIfEmpty) and a `where` using EF.Functions.DateDiffDay â€” there is NO .AsEnumerable()/.ToList()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before the let clauses. The whole composed IQueryable is first materialized by .ToList() on line 860. Therefore the three let collection-projections are part of the server-translated query, not client code. The `where` (808-822) does not reference app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus, so they are projection-only.

WHY IT 500s (data-independent): The final `select new {... app, AppStatus, chkAppStatus ...}` forces EF Core 10 to translate three correlated collection sub-projections. `app` (Approvals.Where().Select(EmployeeId).ToList()) and `AppStatus` (ApprovalUpdates.Where().Select(ApprovalStatus).ToList()) are scalar correlated collections that EF Core 3+ can translate. The killer is `chkAppStatus`: db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(...).GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList() â€” a correlated GroupBy whose selector returns a WHOLE group element via OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault() (not an aggregate/key). EF Core does not translate GroupBy selectors that project full elements per group, and certainly not when embedded as a correlated collection inside an outer projection. EF Core 10 throws InvalidOperationException "could not be translated" at the line-860 .ToList(), BEFORE reading any rows â€” so it 500s on every call regardless of data (even empty tables). EF6 client-evaluated/materialized this exact pattern (legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs lines 764-834, identical modulo DbFunctions.DiffDays -> EF.Functions.DateDiffDay), so it is a true port regression. Severity: HIGH (unconditional 500; the GetMaterialRequisitionApproval endpoint / ApprovalList grid is wholly non-functional).

I am confident enough to mark confirmed without a runtime probe: the GroupBy-select-full-element correlated-collection pattern is a well-established EF Core non-translation, and the sibling method GetMaterialRequisition in the SAME file was already rewritten away from this exact pattern (lines 500-528) â€” strong corroboration that the team treats it as a real break.

breakClass: .ToList()/.ToArray() (or a nested correlated-collection projection) embedded inside a server-side EF Core 10 query â€” either in a `let` clause whose value is consumed by a server `select new {...}`/`where ... .Contains(...)`, or as an inline `Field = (from ... let X=db....ToList() ... select ...).ToList()` sub-collection â€” on an outer IQueryable that has NOT yet crossed an .AsEnumerable()/.ToList() materialization boundary. EF6 client-evaluated these; EF Core 10 throws "could not be translated" (500). Outer queries that are materialized first (var data2 = v.ToList(); from o in data2 ...) or sourced from db.X.AsEnumerable() are SAFE.
why: Source from db.MaterialRequisitionItems with NO materialization boundary; the outer select new embeds let img = db.ItemImages....ToList() AND a nested correlated `bundle` collection that itself contains let bimg = db.ItemImages....ToList(). Both embedded .ToList() materializations sit in the server projection enumerated at line 2270 .ToList(). EF Core 10 cannot translate them. This is the print/PDF path -> hit on every MR print with items -> high. Port regression.
finalFix:
Add `.AsEnumerable()` to the outer `saleitem` source after the joins and where-filter, so the projection (img, bundle, bimg) evaluates client-side. Mirrors the EF6 client-eval behavior; output is byte-identical.

In QuickNetCore/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, change the start of the saleitem query so the base join/filter is materialized before the embedded-.ToList() projection. The cleanest minimal edit is to restructure so the source crosses an .AsEnumerable() boundary before the `select new {...}`. Concretely, move the `where` up and add `.AsEnumerable()`:

    var saleitem = (from b in db.MaterialRequisitionItems
                    join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
                    join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold
                    from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit
                    from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join g in db.ItemBundles on c.ItemID equals g.mainItem into bundle
                    from g in bundle.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join h in db.ItemBrands on b.Make equals h.ItemBrandID into brn
                    from h in brn.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    where b.MaterialRequisition == id && b.ItemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
                    select new { b, c, d, e, h })
                   .AsEnumerable()
                   .Select(x => new {
                       ItemNote = x.b.ItemNote,
                       ItemQuantity = x.b.ItemQuantity,
                       ItemCode = x.c.ItemCode,
                       ItemName = x.c.ItemName,
                       ItemUnit = x.e.ItemUnitName,
                       PartNumber = x.c.PartNumber,
                       ItemRemark = x.b.ItemRemark,
                       CBM = x.d.CBM,
                       Weight = x.d.Weight,
                       img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == x.b.Item).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList(),
                       ItemDescription = x.c.ItemDescription,
                       KeepStock = x.c.KeepStock,
                       Make = x.h.ItemBrandName,
                       ItemID = x.b.Item,
                       FileName = db.ItemImages.Where(a => a.ItemID == x.b.Item).Select(a => a.FileName).FirstOrDefault(),
                       bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                                 join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
                                 join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
                                 join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold2
                                 from dd in scaffold2.DefaultIfEmpty()
                                 join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit
                                 from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                                 where az.mainItem == x.b.Item
                                 select new {
                                     Id = bb.ItemID,
                                     ItemQuantity = (ay.ItemQuantity * x.b.ItemQuantity),
                                     ItemNote = "",
                                     ItemCode = bb.ItemCode,
                                     ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                                     ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName,
                                     PartNumber = bb.PartNumber,
                                     CBM = dd.CBM,
                                     Weight = dd.Weight,
                                     img = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == ay.ItemId).Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID }).ToList(),
                                     KeepStock = bb.KeepStock,
                                     Item = ay.ItemId,
                                     ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName,
                                     ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID,
                                     SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId,
                                     ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic,
                                     ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription
                                 }).ToList(),
                   }).ToList();

Once `.AsEnumerable()` precedes the projection, each `db.ItemImages` / `db.BundleItems` access becomes its own client-side materialized query (LINQ-to-Objects), so the embedded .ToList() calls (img, bimg) and the nested `bundle` sub-collection no longer need server translation. Behavior matches the legacy EF6 client-evaluation exactly. (The LEFT-JOIN nulls for d/e/h are already handled because they were DefaultIfEmpty in the original; AsEnumerable preserves the same null members.) A lighter-touch alternative is to insert `.AsEnumerable()` immediately after the final outer join via a `select new { b, c, d, e, h }` projection then `.AsEnumerable().Select(...)` exactly as shown â€” the essential change is simply that the embedded .ToList() projections must run after a materialization boundary.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED. File: QuickNetCore/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, method generatePdf(long id) (the MR print/PDF builder, called from Download at line 2153; hunter's "MaterialRequisitionPrint" label is approximate but the location is correct).

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. The `saleitem` query at line 2206 sources directly from db.MaterialRequisitionItems (a DbSet/IQueryable on the EF Core DbContext field `db`). The full chain â€” joins (lines 2207-2217), `let img = db.ItemImages.Where(...).Select(im => new pdfItemImg{...}).ToList()` (line 2218), the `select new {...}`, and the nested correlated `bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems ... let bimg = db.ItemImages.Where(...).ToList() ... ).ToList()` (lines 2239-2269) â€” is all composed into the IQueryable. The closing `.ToList()` at line 2270 is the FIRST and ONLY materialization boundary; there is NO `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()` inserted before it. The only `.AsEnumerable()` occurrences in the file (lines 967, 2538, 2576) are unrelated `Max()` aggregates. pdfItemImg (ViewModel/ReportViewModel.cs:760) is a plain non-entity DTO, so `let img` is a correlated-collection projection, not a navigation. Not after a boundary, not a standalone top-level query, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” confirmed. Embedding `.ToList()` on a correlated subquery inside a server-side projection (the `let img ... .ToList()` and inner `let bimg ... .ToList()`, plus the `bundle = (...).ToList()` correlated sub-collection) is exactly the construct EF Core 10 cannot translate; it throws System.InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" at the line-2270 enumeration. EF6 client-evaluated these fragments after streaming base rows; EF Core 10 dropped that automatic client-eval and throws. This is the MR print path â€” every MR line item carries the `let img` (and bundles carry `let bimg`), so it fails on realistic data, not just edge data => high severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs:2205 is byte-for-byte identical and the EF6 app runs fine; the break is purely the EF6â†’EF Core 10 runtime port.

method: MaterialRequisition index/grid action (from b in db.MaterialRequisitions)
breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: Outer 'from b in db.MaterialRequisitions' not materialized before the let; chkAppStatus projected at 858, terminated by .ToList() at 860 -> server-side correlated GroupBy-latest subquery returning entities, untranslatable in EF Core 10 -> 500 on the MR register grid. A DIFFERENT method in this same controller (line 528) already uses a client-side chkAppStatusLookup.TryGetValue, confirming the intended remedy; this query was missed. Legacy has the identical let => port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, GetMaterialRequisitionApproval, insert `.AsEnumerable()` after the correlated `.Where(...)` and before `.GroupBy(...)` so the grouping/ordering/FirstOrDefault run client-side (mirroring EF6's implicit client eval and the already-fixed sibling method at lines 500-528).

Replace lines 804-806:
    let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(a => a.TransEntry == b.MaterialRequisitionId && a.Type == "MaterialRequisition").GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy)
                       .Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault())
                       .ToList().Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList()

with:
    let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(a => a.TransEntry == b.MaterialRequisitionId && a.Type == "MaterialRequisition").AsEnumerable().GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy)
                       .Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault())
                       .Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList()

This forces the filtered ApprovalUpdates rows to be fetched (server-side WHERE preserved), then groups by ApprovedBy, takes the latest row per approver, and projects ApprovalStatus into a List<ApprovalStatus> â€” byte-for-byte the same result the EF6 legacy produced (the now-redundant inner `.ToList()` at line 806 can be dropped as shown, or kept harmlessly). Downstream usage at line 890 (`o.chkAppStatus.Count`, `.Contains(ApprovalStatus.Rejected)`, `.Count()`) is unaffected since the element type and contents are identical.

Caveat: because `b.MaterialRequisitionId` is no longer referenced inside a translatable correlated subquery, EF Core will execute this as an N+1 (one ApprovalUpdates round-trip per outer row materialized at line 860). For a true grid this is acceptable and matches EF6 behavior; if perf matters, prefer the lookup pattern already used at lines 511-522 (materialize the outer rows, then one batched `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(a => a.Type=="MaterialRequisition" && mrIds.Contains(a.TransEntry)).ToList()`, GroupBy(TransEntry).ToDictionary(...) keyed lookup). The minimal `.AsEnumerable()` fix is sufficient to eliminate the 500 and preserve output exactly.
reasoning:
I independently verified the flag and could NOT refute it â€” it is a genuine EF Core 10 port regression that 500s.

LOCATION (note method-name correction): Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, line 804, inside method GetMaterialRequisitionApproval (HttpPost, starts line 711) â€” the MR Approval register grid, NOT a generic "index" action. The hunter's structural claim is otherwise accurate.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The outer query starts at line 790 `var v = (from b in db.MaterialRequisitions ...`. `db` is an EF Core `ApplicationDbContext` (field decl line 32; `db = new ApplicationDbContext()` line 36), and both `MaterialRequisitions` and `ApprovalUpdates` are real `DbSet<>` (ApplicationDbContext.cs lines 342, 355) â€” true server-side IQueryables. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary anywhere between line 790 and the `let chkAppStatus` at line 804; the FIRST materialization boundary is `.ToList()` at line 860. The `let chkAppStatus` subquery is correlated to the outer row via `a.TransEntry == b.MaterialRequisitionId`, so it MUST be composed into the outer query's SQL translation. The inner `.ToList()` (line 806) does NOT create a client boundary for the outer query â€” it is a projected correlated collection that EF Core still has to translate. So the fragment is unambiguously server-side. This is NOT a standalone top-level local, NOT after a materialization boundary, and NOT a safe group-aggregate.

(b) 500 IN EF CORE 10 â€” CONFIRMED, severity HIGH. The subquery shape is: `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(...).GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault())`. This projects a full ENTITY row (non-aggregate, "latest-per-group") out of an IGrouping. EF Core can only translate GroupBy results into SQL when the projection is an aggregate scalar (Count/Sum/Max/etc.) or the grouping key. `g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault()` is neither, so EF Core 10 throws `InvalidOperationException: The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` at query-build/execution time -> HTTP 500. This fails on EVERY load of the grid (translation happens before any data is read), so it is data-independent, not edge-only. (Emptiness is a non-issue: a group is never empty, but that is irrelevant here â€” the failure is translation, not a client-side NRE.)

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs lines 778-780 contains the byte-identical construct inside the same `from b in db.MaterialRequisitions` shape, terminated by the same `.ToList()` at line 834. The port is a faithful 1:1 copy (only EF6 `DbFunctions.DiffDays` -> `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay` differs). EF6 silently client-evaluated the untranslatable group projection; EF Core removed implicit client eval, so the faithful port breaks. Corroborating evidence: a sibling method in this very controller (lines 500-528) was already refactored to a materialized client-side `chkAppStatusLookup` (GroupBy(TransEntry).ToDictionary(... GroupBy(ApprovedBy).Select(grp=>grp.OrderByDescending(CreatedDate).First()) ...)) over `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(...).ToList()` rows â€” proving the intended remedy; the GetMaterialRequisitionApproval method was simply missed.

The hunter's proposed `.AsEnumerable()` insertion is correct and output-preserving.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: h=Items left join by requisition-item's Item FK; Item.PurchasePrice non-nullable decimal. Orphaned/deleted item ref -> h NULL -> 500. Server-side. Legacy identical. MED: dangling item ref.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, GetRequisitionReport, make the two non-nullable decimal reads on the left-joined Items entity `h` null-safe, mirroring EF6's default(decimal)=0:

Line 95 (current):
    ItemQuantity = (h.SubUnitId == g.ItemUnit) ? g.ItemQuantity : g.ItemQuantity / h.ConFactor,
Change to:
    ItemQuantity = (h.SubUnitId == g.ItemUnit) ? g.ItemQuantity : (h != null ? g.ItemQuantity / h.ConFactor : g.ItemQuantity),

Line 96 (current):
    UnitPrice= h.PurchasePrice,
Change to:
    UnitPrice = h != null ? h.PurchasePrice : 0,

Rationale: both h.PurchasePrice and h.ConFactor are non-nullable decimal on Item; when the left join yields NULL h (orphaned g.Item FK or item-less requisition), EF Core 10 throws reading SQL NULL into decimal, whereas EF6 produced 0. The hunter's single-line fix (UnitPrice only) is insufficient because h.ConFactor on line 95 would still crash. Guarding `h != null` for the divide branch also avoids divide-by-zero on the EF6-default 0, preserving the EF6 net result of 0/short-circuit. (Equivalent minimal alternative for line 95: `g.ItemQuantity / (h != null ? h.ConFactor : 1)` to keep a defined numeric result; either preserves the non-crashing legacy outcome since these NULL-h edge rows yielded garbage/0 in EF6 anyway.)
reasoning:
I tried to refute but the break is real. File: QuickNetCore/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs, method GetRequisitionReport, projection at lines 89-110 (UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice at line 96; ItemQuantity reads h.ConFactor at line 95).

SERVER-SIDE: Confirmed. The `select new { ... item = h.ItemCode + "-" + h.ItemName, ItemQuantity = (h.SubUnitId==g.ItemUnit)?g.ItemQuantity:g.ItemQuantity/h.ConFactor, UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice ...}` projection is composed into the IQueryable `v` that is enumerated by `.ToList()` at line 110. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()` boundary before it, so the projection is translated to SQL and run server-side. It is NOT a standalone top-level query and NOT a group-aggregate over an IGrouping â€” the later `group...by` query `v2` (lines 111-140) runs over the already-materialized in-memory list `v`, so the "group never empty" exemption does not apply to this projection.

NULL h is reachable: `h` (Items) is joined via `join h in db.Items on g.Item equals h.ItemID into temp2 from h in temp2.DefaultIfEmpty()` â€” an explicit LEFT JOIN. Entity Item.PurchasePrice is non-nullable decimal (Models/Item.cs line 27) and Item.ConFactor is non-nullable decimal (line 85). g (MaterialRequisitionItems) is also `DefaultIfEmpty()`-joined, and g.Item is a non-nullable long FK with NO enforced referential integrity (ApplicationDbContext configures FKs only for RoleGroup; the model uses manual joins with shadow FKs stripped, lines 710-739). So h is NULL when (a) a MaterialRequisitionItem references a deleted/orphaned Item, or (b) a MaterialRequisition has no items at all (g NULL -> g.Item NULL -> h NULL). The WHERE clause does not exclude these rows (the h.ItemName.Contains(srchtxt) predicate is guarded by `srchtxt=="" ||`, so on the default empty search it never filters NULL h).

500 cause: On a NULL-h row, EF Core 10's result shaper reads SQL NULL into the non-nullable CLR decimal for both UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice AND h.ConFactor (line 95) and throws InvalidOperationException. EF6 silently materialized default(decimal)=0. Verified byte-for-byte port parity against _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/MaterialRequisitionController.cs (legacy lines 84-106 are identical except DbFunctions.DiffDays -> EF.Functions.DateDiffDay), so this is a genuine port regression, not a faithful behavioral port. Severity MED (triggers only on edge data: dangling item ref or item-less requisition), matching the hunter's classification.

Caveat on the hunter's proposed fix: it only guards UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice and MISSES the equally-fatal non-nullable read h.ConFactor on line 95 (g.ItemQuantity / h.ConFactor). A NULL-h row crashes on whichever column the shaper reads first; guarding only PurchasePrice would still 500. The complete output-preserving fix must null-guard both decimal reads (h.PurchasePrice and h.ConFactor), mirroring EF6's default(decimal)=0. h.SubUnitId is long? so the `h.SubUnitId == g.ItemUnit` comparison is itself null-tolerant, and h.ItemCode/h.ItemName are strings (null-safe in concatenation), so those need no change.
